results.title: Signature replay in signatureClaim results in unauthorized claiming of rewards
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit item #13 explicitly identifies that PhiFactory.signatureClaim ignores the chainId field in the signed payload (abi.decode skips/doesn’t validate it), enabling cross-chain signature replay. This is the same function, same root cause, and same attack (reusing a signature from one chain on another). Enforcing chainId binding in signatureClaim (as suggested) would fix both the report’s and the described vulnerability.

results.title: Signature replay in createArt allows to impersonate artist and steal royalties
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit finding #32 pinpoints the same issue in PhiFactory.createArt: the signature does not bind the caller nor the CreateConfig (artist, receiver, fees, etc.), enabling frontrunning/replay to set an attacker-controlled receiver/artist and hijack royalties. It references the same function and mechanism (unsigned CreateConfig + unbound executor), proposes the same fix (bind all critical fields and the executor/nonce/chainId), and an attack that works in the description would work here. Fixing #32 would fix the described vulnerability.

results.title: shareBalance bloating eventually blocks curator rewards distribution
results.reasoning: Yes. Issue #3 in the audit flags CuratorRewardsDistributor.distribute iterating over all curator addresses returned by getCuratorAddresses(credId,0,0), causing gas-limit DoS for credIds with many (historical) curators and permanently locking rewards. The reported mechanism and code path match the described vulnerability: enumeration over the shareBalance-backed address set grows with historical holders (due to zero-balance entries remaining), leading to unbounded iteration and eventual DoS. While the audit does not explicitly call out the zero-balance pruning in Cred._updateCuratorShareBalance, it recognizes the same root manifestation (large/historical curator set enumeration in distribute). Fixing the zero-entry removal would resolve the audit’s DoS as well, so it is the same vulnerability.

results.title: Forced endTime extension in updateArtSettings() allows attacker to mint more tokens
results.reasoning: The described issue is the forced endTime extension in PhiFactory.updateArtSettings(), combined with a > (not >=) check in _validateAndUpdateClaimState that allows same-block backrun minting after the event had ended. None of the audit items mention this mechanism or code path. Findings touching updateArtSettings discuss different issues (e.g., soulbound toggling (#17) and unbounded mintFee (#18)), and while #2 covers unsigned quantity in merkleClaim/signatureClaim, that is a separate flaw whose fix would not address the forced endTime reopening. Therefore, the specific vulnerability was not detected in the audit content.

results.title: Reentrancy Vulnerability Allows Bypass of Cooldown, Leading to Unfair Reward Extraction Through Flash Loan
results.reasoning: The audit report does not detect the specific cred-creation reentrancy that allows overwriting creds before credIdCounter is incremented and exploiting differing bonding curves to drain ETH. The closest item (#27) notes a reentrancy window in _handleTrade for buy/sell, but it neither involves createCred nor the overwrite-before-increment flow, nor the multi-curve price swap that drives the exploit. It also assesses low/medium impact and focuses on sell-then-buy scenarios, not the buy-refund reentrancy used to reenter createCred. Because the code path (createCred + buyShareCred refund), exploit mechanism (overwriting creds), and specific fix (nonReentrant on createCred) are not identified, the report did not detect the same vulnerability.

results.title: Unrestricted changes to token settings allow artists to alter critical features
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit content explicitly flags the same unrestricted-update risks via PhiFactory.updateArtSettings: (1) Issue #17 describes that artists can toggle the soulBounded status at any time via updateArtSettings, retroactively locking transfers—matching the description’s soulbound risk. (2) Issue #16 shows royalties can be set arbitrarily high (unbounded royalty BPS), aligning with the description’s royalty-change concern. Both occur through the same update flow (updateArtSettings calling into royalty update), and the proposed fixes (capping royalties, restricting/removing soulbound/URI updates) would address the same root cause. Thus, the report detected the vulnerability.
